examples.title: Pass by Value Example
examples.description: Demonstrates pass-by-value creating a copy of object
examples.explanation: When Box is passed by value, the copy constructor creates a duplicate. Changes inside modify() affect only the copy, not the original object in main().

examples.title: Pass by Reference Example
examples.description: Demonstrates pass-by-reference allowing modification of original
examples.explanation: The & makes b an alias for the original object. No copy is created. Any modifications inside modify() directly affect the original object in main().

examples.title: Pass by Const Reference Example
examples.description: Demonstrates safest method - efficient read-only access
examples.explanation: const Box& provides efficiency (no copy) and safety (cannot modify). The function can only read data. This is the recommended way to pass large objects for read-only access.

examples.title: Pass by Pointer Example
examples.description: Demonstrates pointer-based object passing with NULL possibility
examples.explanation: Pointer allows NULL values and explicit dereferencing. Use when you need to check for invalid pointers or interface with C code. Arrow operator (->) accesses members.

examples.title: Object Slicing Problem
examples.description: Demonstrates object slicing when passing derived class by value
examples.explanation: When Derived object is passed by value to Base parameter, only the Base part is copied. The derived portion is 'sliced off', losing all derived class data and virtual function behavior.

examples.title: Preventing Object Slicing
examples.description: Shows solution using pointers and references to preserve derived class
examples.explanation: Using pointers or references prevents slicing because they hold the address of the original object. Virtual function calls resolve to derived class implementations at runtime.
